analysis_metadata.specification: Matter Core Specification Section 4.15 - Secure Communications over TCP

property_verdicts.verdict_explanation: FSM explicitly disables MRP reliability on TCP connections with enforced state invariant
FSM handles state transitions correctly but lacks explicit guards preventing send operations during connection break window
FSM enforces session marking with state invariant and verified guards on reconnection
All peer closure transitions include application notification before local closure
Both connection closure paths explicitly call close_all_active_exchanges()
FSM structure enforces backoff - no direct path from closed to reconnection without backoff state
Explicit transition cancels backoff timer on incoming connection request
Guards check message size, connection closes with error on violation
Separate transitions dynamically adjust max size based on transport type
FSM allows dual sessions as spec permits with MAY clause
FSM models DNS-SD reading but not publishing - incomplete model
Property about MRP, FSM models TCP only
Explicit transition for reaping idle connections based on threshold
FSM assumes secure storage but provides no enforcement mechanism for encryption or access control
Keep-alive is optional - FSM allows connections without keep-alive, enabling zombie connections
User timeout enforced with force-close on expiration
Establishment timeout modeled with application notification
Error report sent before connection closure in oversized message handling
State invariant exists but no transition enforces parameter validation when enabling keep-alive
Cryptographic peer identity verification enforced in reconnection guard
FSM allows independent configuration by not enforcing synchronization
FSM structure demonstrates connection dependency through state design

critical_transitions.why_property_holds: Transition to unusable state on peer closure

fsm_analysis.violation_mechanism: No explicit guard preventing send_data trigger between peer_close_notification and actual closure. Race condition window exists where application could attempt send on broken connection.

specification_evidence.quote: a secure session over TCP is unusable when its connection is broken or is closed
specification_evidence.supports_verdict: Confirms session must be unusable immediately on connection break

specification_gap.what_spec_claims: Session is unusable when connection broken
specification_gap.what_spec_fails_to_specify: HOW session usage is prevented (API blocking vs state machine enforcement vs both)
specification_gap.consequence: Implementation-dependent race window between break detection and usage prevention
specification_gap.security_impact: Potential for sending data over broken/hijacked connection before FSM completes transition

steps: Node has active TCP connection with session
Peer closes connection (sends FIN)
Node receives peer_close_notification
FSM begins transition: ConnectionActive → PeerClosedConnection
DURING TRANSITION: Application calls send_data()
No guard prevents this send attempt
Data sent on closing/closed connection (TCP RST or lost)
counterexample_scenario.note: Severity depends on implementation - if application layer blocks sends, no issue; if relies solely on FSM state, race exists

recommendations: Add explicit guard on send_data trigger: connection_status == active && peer_closed == false
Or add state variable 'usable := false' set atomically with peer close notification
Spec should clarify: 'Implementation SHALL prevent session usage immediately upon connection break detection'

attack_path.violation: Function description says 'Store in secure storage' but provides no mechanism to verify or enforce security properties of storage

fsm_analysis.violation_mechanism: FSM assumes secure storage exists and is used but provides no validation, enforcement, or specification of what 'secure' means. Implementation could store resumption state in plaintext file, unencrypted database, or world-readable memory without FSM detecting violation.

specification_evidence.quote: No explicit SHALL requirement for secure storage found in Section 4.15
specification_evidence.context: Specification discusses session retention but not storage security
specification_evidence.gap: No cryptographic or access control requirements specified for resumption state storage

specification_gap.what_spec_claims: Implicit assumption that resumption state is protected
specification_gap.what_spec_fails_to_specify: Explicit security requirements: encryption at rest, access control, integrity protection, key management for storage encryption, storage location permissions
specification_gap.consequence: Each implementation may choose different security levels, creating inconsistent security posture across devices
specification_gap.security_impact: CRITICAL - Attacker with filesystem/memory access can steal resumption state including session keys, enabling full session hijacking without network presence

steps: Node stores resumption state after connection closes
Implementation uses plaintext file in /tmp/ (no spec requirement violated!)
Attacker gains local user access (different user account)
Attacker reads /tmp/ directory (world-readable)
Attacker extracts session keys and peer identity from resumption state
Attacker establishes connection to victim using stolen resumption tokens
FSM verify_peer_identity() passes (attacker has correct credentials)
Attacker hijacks session with full access
counterexample_scenario.note: Resumption state contains session keys - compromise is equivalent to network-level man-in-the-middle

recommendations: Add function precondition: verify_secure_storage_properties() before storing
Define secure storage requirements: AES-256-GCM encryption, OS-level access control (0600 permissions), integrity HMAC
Spec SHALL: 'Resumption state SHALL be stored with encryption at rest using platform secure storage APIs'
Add FSM state variable: storage_security_verified := true as guard condition

attack_path.violation: If keep-alive not configured AND network breaks connection silently (NAT timeout, firewall drop), connection remains in ConnectionActive state forever without transitioning to ConnectionIdle or KeepAliveProbing

fsm_analysis.violation_mechanism: State invariant in ConnectionActive uses conditional: 'keep_alive_configured == true => (parameters present)'. This allows keep_alive_configured == false. With keep-alive disabled, no transition to ConnectionIdle exists (transition guard assumes keep_alive timer). Broken connections never detected, creating zombie connection consuming resources indefinitely.
missing_transitions: ConnectionActive (keep_alive=false, no activity) → ? (no target state for detection without keep-alive)

specification_evidence.quote: Long-lived connections SHOULD be configured to use TCP keep-alive
specification_evidence.supports_verdict: SHOULD = recommendation, not requirement. Spec allows nodes to not configure keep-alive.

specification_gap.what_spec_fails_to_specify: What happens if keep-alive NOT configured? How are broken connections detected? Is there fallback mechanism?
specification_gap.consequence: Nodes following spec may skip keep-alive (SHOULD not SHALL), leading to undetected broken connections
specification_gap.security_impact: HIGH - Resource exhaustion via zombie connections. Attacker forces connection breaks (NAT timeout, firewall rules) causing victim to accumulate dead connections until resource limits reached (file descriptors, memory)

steps: Node establishes TCP connection without configuring keep-alive (spec says SHOULD, not SHALL)
Connection becomes idle - no data transmission for extended period
NAT device times out connection state after 30 minutes (common default)
Network path breaks - packets no longer routed
Node's TCP stack sees no RST or FIN (silently dropped)
Node remains in ConnectionActive state indefinitely
Application attempts to send data → TCP retransmits forever (user timeout prevents infinite retrans)
But if no send attempts, connection stays 'active' forever
Attacker repeats: establish connection, wait for NAT timeout, establish new connection
Victim accumulates zombie connections until file descriptor limit reached
Denial of service - cannot accept new legitimate connections
counterexample_scenario.note: Even if user timeout limits transmission time, connection remains in FSM until send attempted

recommendations: Change SHOULD to SHALL: 'Long-lived connections SHALL configure TCP keep-alive'
Add fallback detection: FSM should include ConnectionActive → IdleNoKeepAlive → ApplicationLayerPing transition
Or: Add mandatory idle timeout even without keep-alive: 'If keep-alive not configured, SHALL close connections idle > X minutes'
FSM should make keep_alive_configured == true a transition guard, not just invariant

attack_path.violation: State invariant says 'keep_alive_configured == true => (parameters present)' but no transition action validates parameters before setting flag. Implementation can set flag without parameters, violating invariant.

fsm_analysis.violation_mechanism: State invariant is declaration, not enforcement. FSM lacks validation transition that checks: IF keep_alive_configured THEN verify(has_tcp_keep_alive_time && has_tcp_keep_alive_interval && has_tcp_keep_alive_probes). Implementation can violate invariant without FSM detecting it.

specification_evidence.quote: If TCP keep-alive is used, three parameters SHALL be configured
specification_evidence.context: Conditional requirement: IF keep-alive THEN parameters required
specification_evidence.supports_verdict: Specification requires parameters when keep-alive enabled

specification_gap.what_spec_claims: Three parameters SHALL be configured if keep-alive used
specification_gap.what_spec_fails_to_specify: WHEN validation occurs (before enabling? at connection time?), WHO validates (application? stack? protocol layer?), WHAT happens if parameters missing (error? disable keep-alive? use defaults?)
specification_gap.consequence: Implementation could enable keep-alive with missing/invalid parameters, causing undefined behavior
specification_gap.security_impact: MEDIUM - Incorrect keep-alive behavior may fail to detect broken connections or detect too aggressively causing false positives and connection churn

steps: Implementation sets keep_alive_configured := true
Fails to set tcp_keep_alive_interval (programming error)
FSM has no validation transition to catch this
Connection established with incomplete keep-alive config
Idle timer expires, transitions to KeepAliveProbing
send_keep_alive_probe() attempts to use tcp_keep_alive_interval
Value is undefined/null → probe timing incorrect
Either: Probes sent too fast (DoS peer), or too slow (miss broken connections)
counterexample_scenario.note: Not directly exploitable but creates operational failures

recommendations: Add validation transition: ValidateKeepAliveConfig state between Initial and ConnectionEstablishing
Guard on entering ConnectionActive: validate_keep_alive_parameters() == true OR keep_alive_configured == false
Function: validate_keep_alive_parameters() { return keep_alive_configured => (has_time && has_interval && has_probes) }
Reject connection establishment if validation fails

critical_transitions.why_unverifiable: Guard checks DNS-SD but no transition shows DNS-SD publishing/updating

fsm_analysis.incomplete_modeling: FSM models capability checking (reading DNS-SD) but not capability advertisement (writing/publishing DNS-SD). Cannot verify if node actually publishes 'T' record when it supports TCP.

specification_evidence.quote: TCP support for this communication mechanism MUST be communicated via the device discovery mechanisms described in Section 4.3
specification_evidence.context: Requires publishing TCP capability via DNS-SD
specification_evidence.supports_verdict: Property requires both reading AND writing DNS-SD

gaps_identified: FSM missing: Node initialization transition that publishes DNS-SD TXT record with 'T' key
FSM missing: Transition handling TCP capability enable/disable that updates DNS-SD
FSM missing: Error handling if DNS-SD publishing fails
recommendations: Add state: DNSSDPublishing with transition from Initial
Add guard on ConnectionEstablishing: dns_sd_published == true

critical_findings: PROP_014: Resumption state security not enforced - session hijacking via storage compromise
PROP_015: Optional keep-alive enables zombie connections - resource exhaustion attack
PROP_019: Keep-alive parameter validation missing - undefined behavior
PROP_002: Race condition on connection break - potential send on broken connection

fsm_quality_assessment.notes: FSM models happy paths well but lacks enforcement mechanisms for security-critical invariants. Several properties rely on assumptions rather than verification.

summary.next_action: Recommend FSM updates to add validation transitions for PROP_014, PROP_015, PROP_019
